title: How the Pieces Move
summary: Meet every piece on the board

steps.title: Welcome
steps.text: Chess is played on 64 squares by 16 pieces per side. White always moves first. You'll meet every piece in turn — the pawn, knight, bishop, rook, queen, and king.

steps.title: The Pawn
steps.text: Pawns march forward one square at a time — or two on their very first move. They capture diagonally. A pawn that reaches the far side promotes into any piece you choose. Small, but decisive.

steps.prompt: Push the pawn two squares forward to claim the center.
steps.hint: On its first move, a pawn may jump two squares. Try e4.

steps.title: The Knight
steps.text: Knights leap in an L-shape — two squares one way, then one at a right angle. They're the only piece that can jump over others. Tricky to see, deadly in the right spot.

steps.prompt: Develop the knight toward the center. Play Nf3 or Ng3.
steps.hint: Knight on h1 can reach f2 or g3.

steps.title: The Bishop
steps.text: Bishops glide along diagonals as far as they like. Each bishop stays on one color forever — one controls light squares, the other dark. They love open positions.

steps.title: The Rook
steps.text: Rooks move in straight lines — along ranks and files. They become monsters in the endgame and love open files.

steps.title: The Queen
steps.text: The queen combines rook and bishop — moving any distance along ranks, files, or diagonals. She's the most powerful piece. Don't bring her out too early; she can be chased.

steps.title: The King
steps.text: The king steps one square in any direction. Protect him at all costs — if he's checkmated, the game ends. In the endgame, activate him: he's worth about three pawns in the attack.
